coredata.dc:title: Highly selective carbendazim fungicide sensing performance of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots encapsulated aminated-UiO-66 zirconium metal-organic framework electrocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Velmurugan, Sethupathi
Traiwatcharanon, Pranlekha
Jiwanti, Prastika Krisma
Cheng, Shu-Hua
Wongchoosuk, Chatchawal
coredata.dc:description: A well-known benzimidazole insecticide called carbendazim (CBM) is used in agriculture to both prevent and treat fungal plant diseases. The health of people is seriously threatened by residual CBM in water regions and food. In this study, we designed and prepared nitrogen-doped-carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) encapsulated in zirconium metal-organic framework (MOF) UiO-66-NH2 (N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2) electrocatalyst by facile in-situ encapsulation technique. The as-prepared electrocatalyst has been used for selective electrochemical detection of carbendazim (CBM) fungicide. The N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 coated screen printed electrode (SPCE) revealed a highly selective CBM detection ability with detection parameters of 0.02 – 126 µM and 5.8 nM as detection range and detection limit respectively. The highly selective CBM sensor performance of the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst was >95 % and the real-time minimum CBM retrieval response in vegetable and environmental water samples was 96 % and 97 % respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
UiO-66-NH2 metal-organic framework
Carbendazim fungicide
Electrochemical sensor
Water region
Vegetable samples

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrode fabrication and real sample preparation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Research Council of Thailand ( N42A650234 ). The authors immensely thank the Universitas Airlangga for the funding for this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pictorial representation for synthesis process of electrocatalysts.

HRTEM image (A) and EDX spectra (B) of N-CQDs (insets: illumination of prepared N-CQDs under UV irradiation and a pie chart of elemental ratio). TEM images of UiO-66-NH2 (C-D) and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 (E-F) electrocatalysts (insets: optical images of corresponding aqueous dispersions).
XRD spectra (A), BET isotherm (B), FTIR spectra (C), and Raman spectra (D) of UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalysts.
XPS survey (A), Zr 3d (B), C 1s (C), N 1s (D), and O 1s (E) spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalysts.

EIS plots (A) and CV voltammograms (B) of bare SPCE, UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE performed in 0.1 M KCl aqueous solution containing 1 mM ferricyanide. CV voltammograms (C) of bare SPCE (a, d), UiO-66-NH2/SPCE (b, e), and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE (c, f) in pH 7 buffer with the absence and presence of 50 µM CBM and its corresponding bar diagram (D).

(A) CV voltammograms of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE in pH 7 in the presence of various CBM concentrations and (B) related linear plot of CBM concentration vs. current. (C) CV voltammograms of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE in the presence of 50 µM CBM at various pH buffers and (D) related linear plot of current vs. pH vs. peak potential.
(A) DPV responses of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE in pH 7 with several additions of CBM concentrations from 0.02 to 126 µM and (B) related linear plot of CBM concentration vs. current.
(A) Interference study relative error bar diagram and (B) long-term stability of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE in the presence of 10 µM CBM.
Comparison of CBM sensing ability with previous literature reports.

head.ce:title: Highly selective carbendazim fungicide sensing performance of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots encapsulated aminated-UiO-66 zirconium metal-organic framework electrocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Engineering, Faculty of Advanced Technology and Multidiscipline, Universitas Airlangga, 60115 Surabaya, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Advanced Technology and Multidiscipline

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aNanotechnology Engineering, Faculty of Advanced Technology and Multidiscipline, Universitas Airlangga, 60115 Surabaya, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok 10900, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok 10900, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, National Chi Nan University, Puli, Nantou 545, Taiwan, ROC
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Applied Chemistry, National Chi Nan University, Puli, Nantou 545, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A well-known benzimidazole insecticide called carbendazim (CBM) is used in agriculture to both prevent and treat fungal plant diseases. The health of people is seriously threatened by residual CBM in water regions and food. In this study, we designed and prepared nitrogen-doped-carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) encapsulated in zirconium metal-organic framework (MOF) UiO-66-NH2 (N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2) electrocatalyst by facile in-situ encapsulation technique. The as-prepared electrocatalyst has been used for selective electrochemical detection of carbendazim (CBM) fungicide. The N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 coated screen printed electrode (SPCE) revealed a highly selective CBM detection ability with detection parameters of 0.02 – 126 µM and 5.8 nM as detection range and detection limit respectively. The highly selective CBM sensor performance of the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst was >95 % and the real-time minimum CBM retrieval response in vegetable and environmental water samples was 96 % and 97 % respectively.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides have been widely employed in agricultural production to control pests or weeds because of their broad-spectrum activity and inexpensive production costs [1]. Pesticides known as fungicides work to eradicate or stop the development of fungi and fungi spores. They are effective against rusts, mildews, and other plant-damaging fungi [2]. One of the best fungicides for protecting crops against pathogen attacks is carbendazim (CBM), which is used as a preventative measure before and after harvesting vegetables, fruits, and other crops [3]. When CBM is applied to soil, the compound's benzimidazole ring displays long-term persistent and outstanding action. It is also tough to break and maintains a gradual deterioration [4]. As a result, CBM residues could be widely dispersed in the air, water, and soil, endangering human health and the preservation of the environment [5]. Consuming residual CBM could result in poisoning, which is primarily exhibited by harm to the DNA, nervous system, organs, and infertility and may potentially be life-threatening [6]. Developing a sensitive, precise, and portable CBM trace quantity measuring method in vegetables and environmental samples has therefore gained real significance for environmental contamination and human health [7].

Up to date, various methods have been proposed for CBM detection including fluorescence [8], liquid chromatography-triple quadrupole mass spectrometry [9], surface-enhanced Raman spectrometry [10], and electrochemical sensor [11]. Due to its quick processing time, high sensitivity, compactness and portability, cheap cost, straightforward operation, and good selectivity, the electrochemical sensor has emerged as one of the most promising detection techniques [12–17]. However, finding potential electrocatalysts with selective interaction toward CBM is highly desirable. Due to their intriguing physiochemical properties, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which are built by a spacing assembly of metal ions/clusters with organic ligands by coordination bonds, have drawn a lot of attention in the fields of gas storage and adsorption, catalysis, chemical separations, energy storage, and light harvesting [18–20]. However, most of the MOF-based nanomaterials are also electronic insulators, which is a critical drawback for electrode materials since it causes electrode polarization and suppresses electrochemical dynamics, which lowers the efficiency of electrochemically active nanomaterials [21].

In this regard, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an exciting member of the carbon nanomaterials family and a promising carbon nanostructure. It exhibits optical and electrical properties due to the quantum confinement phenomena. The CQDs also display advantageous characteristics like biocompatibility, lower toxicity, and chemical stability [22,23]. The abundant -COOH (carboxyl) groups seen in small-sized CQDs can preferentially align with metal clusters in the MOFs, similar to the prevalence coordination between the linker and metal cluster in the MOF [24]. By retaining both the traits of the MOF matrix and their distinctive qualities, the encapsulated CQDs can provide a synergistic effect [25]. In terms of doping, the intrinsic characteristics of the CQDs, such as their electrical, optical, local chemistry, and surface reactivity, can be influenced by heteroatoms especially nitrogen (N) atoms [26]. Moreover, the N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) and their composites have been tailored for the selective detection of various antibiotics and biomolecules [27]. A glassy carbon electrode-coated N-CQDs showed selective and simultaneous detection of paracetamol and H2O2 in the pharmaceutical analysis [28] and a facile-prepared N-CQDs/Cu2O electrocatalyst exhibited outstanding and sensitive aspirin detection activity in berries [29].

Though many advanced methods for CQD preparation most of them fall short due to expensive starting materials, convoluted processes, challenging synthesis conditions, and energy-intensive machinery [30–32]. A workable method for creating water-soluble CQDs is hydrothermal preparation, which uses a variety of inexpensive organic materials as the carbon source [33,34]. An in-situ encapsulated blue and yellow light-emitting CQDs in the ZIF-8 MOF (BYCD@ZIF-8) has been applied for ratiometric glutathione detection in the presence of Cu2+ and showed satisfactory responses in fruit samples [35]. Similarly, an in-situ encapsulation synthesized N-CQDs in porphyrin MOF (N-CQDs@PCN-222) was employed for smartphone-integrated colorimetric detection of glyphosate in environmental water and cereal samples [36]. Zhang et al. synthesized amine-CQDs@UiO-67 catalyst visa post-synthesis incorporation of amine-CQDs in UiO-67, which exhibited highly selective fluorescent detection characteristics of 2,4-dinitrophenol [37]. Yang et al. prepared an amine-CQDs@UiO-66 catalyst followed by a similar post-synthesis incorporation method used for highly selective fluorescence sensing of 4-nitrophenol and achieved a detection limit of 3.5 nM [38]. Moreover, the post-synthesis modified N-CQDs@UiO-66/PVA hydrogel catalyst was deployed for ratiometric detection of pethidine opioid analgesic in human plasma and achieved a minimum recovery of 90.8 % [39].

Herein, the research reports an in-situ encapsulation of N-CQDs in zirconium UiO-66-NH2 MOF (N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2) electrocatalyst via a facile preparation method and was utilized for highly selective electrochemical sensing of CBM fungicide which has not been reported elsewhere. To confirm the synergy, various microscopic and spectroscopic characterizations of the as-prepared N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalysts have been conducted. Furthermore, the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE electrode exhibits exceptional electrochemical performance for the detection of CBM and is superior to other electrodes in this regard. Thus, the suggested sensor materials (N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2) are reliable, have strong selectivity, and are highly electrically active, including the capacity to detect CBM.

Zirconium(IV) chloride (ZrCl4) anhydrous (99.99 %), 2-amino-terephthalic acid (BDC-NH2, 99 %), methyl benzimidazol-2-ylcarbamate (CBM, 97 %), and many other chemicals were procured from Sigma Aldrich, Thailand. For the study, the monobasic and dibasic potassium phosphate salts were dissolved in DI water (18 MΩ cm). Using diluted HCl and KOH solutions, the buffer's acidic and basic conditions were adjusted.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the electrocatalysts were recorded on JEOL JSM-7610F field-emission SEM (FESEM) and JEM 2100Plus multipurpose TEM instruments respectively. Moreover, the crystalline nature and elemental composition of the electrocatalysts were observed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies using Bruker (Singapore) and Axis Ultra DLD (Kratos analytical, Manchester UK) instruments respectively. The nitrogen (N2) isotherm and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the electrocatalysts was measured in a Micromeritics 3Flex analyzer. The functional moieties of the electrocatalysts were analyzed by Shimadzu IRTracer-100 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer and the Raman vibrational modes were obtained using Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution Confocal Raman Microscope. The voltammetric and impedance studies of various electrodes were performed in CHI 600E CH instruments.

First, 25 mL of deionized (DI) water is used to dissolve 3 g of citric acid and 1 g of urea, which is then agitated for 15 min. After that, the solution went through 15 min of ultrasonic oscillations in a bath sonicator. The flask was hydrothermally heated at 180 °C for 6 h and allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The deep yellowish-brown product produced during the reaction is centrifuged for 15 min at a speed of 10,000 rpm to get the supernatant. The furnace was then allowed to cool naturally. For 24 h, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis tube (MWCO: 1000 Da) against ultrapure water to remove small unreacted precursor molecules. The obtained products will be further concentrated to make 5 mg/mL of N-CQDs in deionized water.

To synthesize Zr-MOF UiO-66-NH2, about 116.5 mg of ZrCl4 and 91 mg of BDC-NH2 linker were dissolved in 20 mL DMF. Then 25 mL of DI water and 1.5 mL of concentrated HCl were added to the mixture. After stirring for an hour, a clear solution was heated at 90 °C for 24 h. The obtained pale-yellow precipitate was washed with DMF and ethanol several times by centrifugation and oven-dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The UiO-66-NH2 was obtained as a pale yellowish powder sample.

The same procedure was also followed for the synthesis of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst with the addition of 1.5 mL of prepared N-CQDs to the precursor solution. The color of the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 became slightly darker than the UiO-66-NH2 due to the added N-CQDs. Later the obtained crude was washed with DMF and ethanol many times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The resulting powder sample was brighter in color compared to the pristine UiO-66-NH2. The detailed synthesis process of the electrocatalysts is represented in Scheme .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode fabrication and real sample preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalysts were ultrasound dispersed at 2 mg/mL in DI water at first. Then about 7 µL of dispersed solutions were drop-casted on SPCE and dried at 50 °C in a vacuum oven. The electrodes were washed every time with DI water before and after use electrochemically. The typical three-electrode system consists of electrocatalysts with modified or unmodified SPCE (area of 0.07 cm²) as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode respectively.

For the real sample inspection, vegetables such as lettuce, tomatoes, and soybeans were obtained from local markets in Bangkok. The collected samples were chopped and mashed individually in a mortar, 1 h sonicated with an adequate amount of ethanol, and filtered with a membrane 0.2 µm filter. The environmental samples like paddy water, lake water, and river water were collected from Kasetsart University Kamphaeng Saen campus, Nong Bon Lake, and Chao Phraya River in Bangkok respectively. All the real samples were filtered separately and centrifuged. The reserved supernatants were diluted 100 times with pH 7 buffer and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Then known concentrations of CBM were spiked to analyze by the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method.

The morphology and particle size of the electrocatalysts were evaluated using FESEM observations. As shown in Fig. S1, both the UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalysts reveal nanoparticle morphology in FESEM recordings. To further investigate the morphology and nature of electrocatalysts including the synthesized N-CQDs, the TEM studies were also carried out. It can be seen from Fig. 1 A that the particle size of N-CQDs is about 4 nm and the d spacing (0.32 nm) is well matching the 002 plane of the carbon phase. Furthermore, the inset image shows the photograph of the N-CQDs exhibiting blue emission under UV illumination representing the successful synthesis of quantum dots. In addition, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. 1B) confirm the N doping of CQDs, and the elemental constitutions were given as a pie chart in the inset. The nanoparticle morphology of pristine UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 catalysts was once again confirmed by TEM images as displayed in Fig. 1C–F. The N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 shows stable aqueous dispersion (insets) compared to the pristine MOF and this can be owing to the N-CQDs particles.

To validate the crystalline phase of the electrocatalysts and to look into the effect of encapsulation, XRD patterns have been acquired for the samples. Fig. 2 A reveals the XRD patterns of the catalysts before and after encapsulation of N-CQDs in the UiO-66-NH2 MOF structure. The UiO-66-NH2 XRD patterns were adhered to the previously reported works [40,41]. To observe the changes after N-CQDs encapsulation in MOF three major peaks at 7.59°, 8.72°, and 25.92° related to the (111), (002), and (006) planes of the MOF were noticed. At first, the intensity of UiO-66-NH2 patterns was decreased due to the added N-CQDs, and then the 2θ values of the peaks were lowered to 7.48°, 8.64°, and 25.74° due to the interaction between encapsulated N-CQDs particles and shell UiO-66-NH2 MOF. Thus, the result confirms the successful N-CQDs encapsulation in UiO-66-NH2.

The N2 adsorption and desorption experiment was used to characterize the BET surface area and pore volume of the catalysts. Fig. 2B depicts that both the electrocatalysts exhibit type I isotherm curves with no presence of a hysteresis loop [42]. The recorded BET surface area of the UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 are 702 m²/g and 687 m²/g respectively. The surface area of obtained UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles is comparatively higher than previously reported works such as 554 and 671 m2/g [43,44]. The decrease in surface area of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was expected since the N-CQDs were encapsulated in the MOF but not over the surface. Moreover, the decrease in pore volume from 0.20 cm³/g (UiO-66-NH2) to 0.17 cm³/g (N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2) once again evidence the encapsulation of N-CQDs inside the UiO-66-NH2 MOF architecture.

FTIR spectroscopy was carried out to explore the potential functional groups and chemical bonds of the electrocatalysts and to obtain additional proof of their successful synthesis. In common, both the UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 2C) show peaks at 3200–3350 cm−1 NH and OH bonds stretching vibrations [45,46]. The UiO-66-NH2 exhibits notable peaks at 1560, 1425, 1385, 1256, 1161, 964, 767, 657, and 478 cm−1 are caused by the CC stretching, CN stretching, NH wagging, and Zr-O vibrations [45,42]. Moreover, the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 catalyst exhibited some additional peaks in the FTIR spectra at 1045, 1082, and 1655 cm−1 are related to the COC (alkoxy) stretching, NH bending, and CO/CN stretching vibrations respectively. Also, two significant peaks were noted at 2930 and 2975 cm−1 corresponding to the vibrations of CH functional groups [47,48]. These additional functional group features have risen from the added N-CQDs in the MOF due to the existing interaction [25,26].

Furthermore, Raman spectra of the electrocatalysts are displayed in Fig. 2D. Both samples showed vibrational peaks of UiO-66-NH2 zirconium MOF similar to the previous reports [49]. The D and G bands of the N-CQDs carbon phase were not visible in the spectra due to the low doping amount of N-CQDs and the high background signal intensity of UiO-66-NH2 MOF. Hence, the Raman spectra of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 mimic the spectra of UiO-66-NH2 but with high intensity. XPS was used to examine the oxidation states and surface chemical compositions of the electrocatalyst samples. In Fig. 3 A, the survey scan unravels the elemental composition of the pristine UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalysts and the obtained elemental ratios were given as inset. Moreover, the figure showed four primary peaks for UiO-66-NH2 (and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2) samples attributed to Zr 3d, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s with energies of 183.26 (183.40), 284.85 (285.02), 399.66 (399.80), and 532.16 (532.30) eV, respectively.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 are shown in Fig. 3B to E in order to better illustrate the functional group distribution. The Zr 3d spectra of both electrocatalysts exhibit two distinct peaks because the Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 orbitals are from Zr4+ oxidation states (Fig. 3B). In addition, the Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 peaks of UiO-66-NH2 and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 were recorded at 183.17, 185.68, 183.33, and 185.70 eV respectively [50]. As seen in Fig. 3C, the C 1s spectra of UiO-66-NH2 can be divided into three parts: the CC group (284.85 eV), the CN group (286.24 eV), and the OCO group (289.06 eV). The binding energies of these functional groups in the C 1s spectra of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 were located at 285.02 (CC), 286.37 (CN), and 289.16 (OCO) eV [50]. The observed binding energies’ shift in the Zr 3d and C 1s spectra after encapsulation of N-CQDs directly implies a strong presence of interaction between the N-CQDs and UiO-66-NH2 [25,26]. The N 1s spectra of the electrocatalysts are shown in Fig. 3D and the observed two deconvoluted peaks in each at 399.9, 401.16, 399.80, and 401.14 eV are associated with the -NH2 attached to the BDC's phenyl ring (NH2) and protonated amidogens (NH3 +) respectively [50]. Moreover, the O 1s spectra of the samples reveal three deconvoluted peaks for the ZrOZr, ZrOC, and OCO groups at 530.65, 532.16, 533.70, 530.98, 532.31, and 533.68 eV respectively [50,51]. The results confirm that strong interaction exists in the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst.

The interface (electrode-electrolyte) characteristics of catalysts-modified electrodes were assessed using the EIS experiment. Usually, the impedance value will be measured in EIS to better know the conductivity behavior of an electrode. In the Nyquist plot, the resistance of charge transfer (Rct) value of an electrode surface is measured by the diameter of the semicircle portion which is known as an impedance. Herein, the electrodes were immersed and EIS was performed in 0.1 M KCl aqueous solution containing 1 mM ferricyanide by impedance potential technique. From Fig. 4 A, calculations reveal that the Rct values for N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, and bare SPCE are 39, 227, and 393 Ω respectively. The lower Rct value of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE indicates that N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 have greater conductivity, which can accelerate the rate of electron transfer between the electrode and the electrolyte. Thus, during the CBM detection procedure, N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE can facilitate quick electron transfer.

Using the typical ferricyanide system, cyclic voltammetry (CV) examined the electrochemical characteristics of various modified electrodes. The CV was also tested in a similar 0.1 M KCl aqueous solution containing 1 mM ferricyanide. At a scan rate of 100 mV/s, a distinct redox couple associated with the ferricyanide system was seen on all modified electrodes (Fig. 4B). The calculated Ep (peak-to-peak separation) of the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, and bare SPCE are 98, 96, and 126 mV respectively. Moreover, the observed current was higher for N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE than for other electrodes. Additionally, the electron transfer abilities of electrodes can be listed in the following order: bare SPCE < UiO-66-NH2/SPCE < N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, which proved that the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst has better electron transfer characteristics than the counterparts.

The CV technique was used to analyze the electrochemical characteristics of CBM at various electrodes in phosphate buffer pH 7.0 with a scan rate of 100 mV/s. Fig. 4C represents the CV responses of the bare SPCE, UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE electrodes in the absence and presence of 50 µM CBM. The CV sensing of CBM at the electrodes reveals one oxidation peak (O1) followed by a reduction peak (R1). The observation of the O1 peak is due to the methylcarbamate and benzimidazole radicals production reaction and the R1 peak is because of the consecutive dimer formation of the produced two benzimidazole radicals followed by reduction [3,6]. The high-intensity O1 anodic peaks of CBM for the bare SPCE, UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE electrodes were noted at 0.873, 0.866, and 0.860 V oxidation potentials respectively. On the other hand, the observed current values of bare SPCE, UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, and N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE electrodes are 5.2, 11.9, and 22.2 µA respectively. The obvious oxidation current differences can be seen in the relevant bar diagram (Fig. 4D). In comparison, the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE exhibited lower oxidation potential and higher current values than the other electrodes. Thus, the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst drop casted SPCE showed better CBM sensing performance, and hence from here on the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE will be used for all the CBM detection activity.

On the electrochemical catalysis of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE, the effect of CBM concentration was investigated. For this, the electrode was inserted in pH 7.0 consisting of various concentrations of CBM injected and was tested at 100 mV/s scan rate. According to Fig. 5 A, CV curves for N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE for various CBM concentrations ranging from 10 µM to 60 µM, the oxidation currents increase linearly as the CBM concentration rises. The linearity was confirmed from the correlation plot in Fig. 5B by the obtained equation of Ipa (µA) = 0.4713 [CBM/µM] + 2.6066 with the coefficient of R2 = 0.9978. These findings demonstrate the viability of the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE as a promising platform for CBM electrochemical sensing.

The electrochemical behavior of CBM at N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE at various pH levels was examined to better know the pH effect. Fig. 5C shows the CV curves of 50 µM CBM at N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE at various buffer pH from 3.0 to 9.0. The graphic shows that anodic potential shifts to a lower potential with an increase in pH, indicating the involvement of H+ ions in the sensing process. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5D the slope obtained from the calibration plot is about 56 pH/mV, which is similar to the Nernstian theoretical value of 59 pH/mV. This suggests that the number of electrons and protons involved in the electrode process is equal. On this basis, the mechanism of the CBM sensor involves the oxidation of CBM followed by dimer formation and reduction which is similar to the literature reports [3,6]. A higher peak current was seen at pH 7.0 based on the effects of pH. Thus, a pH of 7.0 was employed for the CBM sensing throughout the research.

Similarly, the electrochemical sensing of CBM was thoroughly explored at UiO-66-NH2/SPCE by examining its behavior at various sweep levels. To study this, the electrode was inserted in pH 7.0 consisting of 50 µM CBM and was tested at diverse scan rates. From Fig. S2A, a higher anodic peak current was seen with scan rates ranging from 30 to 300 mV/s. Moreover, Fig. S2B depicts the correlation between the anodic peak current (Ipa) and the scan rate. Ipa (µA) = 0.0844 [scan rate] (mV/s) + 11.3333 is determined to be the linear regression equation, and the correlation coefficient is 0.996. According to the aforementioned findings, CDM is electrochemically oxidized at the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE by following a conventional adsorption-controlled process.

The DPV approach, as opposed to the CV method, provides superior resolution with smaller analyte quantities. As a result, herein the DPV method was used to quantitatively determine the CBM. Fig. 6 A displays the DPV voltammograms obtained from various CBM concentrations from 0.02 to 126 µM in a pH of 7.0 buffer. The peak current increased as a result of the increased CBM quantity. Moreover, the linear equation of Ipa (µA) = 0.5115 [CBM/µM] + 0.2425 and the linearity coefficient of R2 = 0.9971 were obtained at first in the calibration plot (Fig. 6B). The detection limit was calculated on the first slope of the lower concentrations’ linear equation. The detection limit is estimated from a 3SD/m relation.

Here, the SD represents the standard deviation of the blank and m denotes the slope of the linear addition plot. The estimated CBM detection limit of N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE in pH 7.0 buffer is 0.0058 µM (5.8 nM). In comparison to previously published electrocatalysts (Table 1 ), the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst exhibits higher sensitivity and satisfactory CBM sensing performance, according to the obtained detection limit and micro-nanomolar detection range.

The DPV response of various potential interfering species was tested with CBM to examine the anti-interference ability of the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE sensor. The electrode was DPV performed in pH 7 consisting of 10 µM CBM alone and with five folds of mancozeb (MNZ), imidacloprid (IMC), diazinon (DAZ), methyl parathion (MPT), diuron (DIU), atrazine (ATZ), urea and fifty folds of Na+, Cu2+, SO4 2−, NO3 − ions. Fig. 7 A portrays the relative error results of the interfering species to CBM and the CBM signaling response deviation was lower than 3 %. This implies the selective CBM oxidation but not much of the added interferences. Henceforth, the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE sensor possesses greater selectivity towards CBM than other chemically similar pesticides and fungicides.

The precision studies of the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE sensor such as reproducibility, repeatability, and long-term stability were examined to establish the reliability and electrode stability. As displayed in Fig. S3A, the repeatability performance of the sensor was validated by ten consecutive DPV runs using a single electrode in pH 7 with 10 µM CBM. Similarly, the reproducibility analysis was evaluated by seven electrodes in pH 7 with 10 µM CBM as seen in Fig. S3B. Moreover, the long-term stability of the electrode has been monitored for 24 days with a term interval of 4 days in pH 7 with 10 µM CBM (Fig. 7B). The end-calculated RSD values for repeatability, reproducibility, and end-stability for long-term stability were 2.7 %, 1.1 %, and 95.4 % respectively. Overall, the N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE sensor has reliable CBM detection performances and electrode-material stability.

The CBM sensing feasibility in real-time was tested in vegetables and environmental samples. The details of real sample preparations are mentioned in Section 2.5. The direct addition method was employed for the CBM detection in real samples. By spiking a predetermined quantity of CBM into the sample solutions, the recovery test was performed. The standard calibration curve in Fig. 6B was used to determine the CBM concentrations added to the prepared samples. The estimated found concentrations and the appropriate recovery values are tabulated. As can be seen in Table S1, the CBM recoveries in vegetables and environmental samples range from 96.3–101.7 % and 97.5–100.5 % respectively. Moreover, the results exhibit high sensitivity CBM recovery compared to the high-performance liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detector (HPLC-UV) counterpart. These findings show that the suggested N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE sensor has good accuracy for detecting CBM in real samples.

In this work, a highly selective N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE sensor was proposed to detect the CBM fungicide. The facile-prepared N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2 electrocatalyst exhibited high aqueous stability due to the in-situ N-CQDs encapsulation in the UiO-66-NH2 MOF structure. Moreover, the electrocatalysts also reached excellent conductivity and CBM sensing activity. The N-CQDs@UiO-66-NH2/SPCE shows nano-micromolar CBM detection ability with a low detection limit of 0.0058 µM. In addition, the anti-inference test outperformed other possible fungicide and herbicide interferences from CBM sensing activity. Most remarkably, the real-time detection was carefully performed in different vegetables and environmental samples which ensured the potential applications of the electrocatalysts.

Sethupathi Velmurugan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Pranlekha Traiwatcharanon: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Prastika Krisma Jiwanti: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – original draft. Shu-Hua Cheng: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Chatchawal Wongchoosuk: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (N42A650234). The authors immensely thank the Universitas Airlangga for the funding for this research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2024.143911.
